ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a paneer food product node but no specific node for paneer tikka preparations. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cheese, fresh (unripened) — used as closest parent for paneer; no specific paneer tikka code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated paneer tikka entry. A0CKP (fresh unripened cheese) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has a paneer substance code but no specific tikka/achari/hariyali preparation code.

main_ingredients: achari spice mix (for achari variant: mustard seeds, fennel, nigella, fenugreek, dried mango powder)
green herb paste (for hariyali variant: mint, coriander, green chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of paneer tikka consists of approximately 250 g, comprising 4–6 cubes of marinated grilled paneer with vegetables such as bell peppers and onion.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Paneer Tikka preparations
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for paneer (raw) and grilled paneer preparations. Paneer tikka values are adapted from the grilled paneer entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Indian-style grilled paneer (FDC ID 2346428) to account for marinade ingredients (yogurt, spices, oil). Macros represent a composite estimate for the marinated-and-grilled product per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — paneer / fresh cheese analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for paneer tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 27 is estimated by analogy with fresh paneer / Indian cottage cheese, which has a published GI of approximately 27 (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). The marinade and grilling process do not substantially alter the glycemic response given the low carbohydrate content of the dish. GL per serving calculated as: (27 × 14.5 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 3.9, rounded to 4.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices, salt, and yogurt
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of paneer tikka contributes approximately 1050 mg sodium, which represents about 46% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 27) and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer tikka has a low GI of approximately 27 and provides only 14.5 g of carbohydrate per standard 250 g serving, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though the fat content from full-fat paneer should be monitored in the context of overall caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from full-fat paneer and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 250 g serving delivers approximately 24.5 g of saturated fat, exceeding the American Heart Association's recommended daily limit of 13 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume paneer tikka in moderation and consider lower-fat paneer variants or smaller portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Tikka (including its Achari and Hariyali variants) is a popular North Indian appetiser and main course dish made from cubes of paneer — a fresh, non-melting Indian cottage cheese — that are marinated in spiced yogurt and then grilled or cooked in a tandoor (clay oven). The standard tikka marinade includes yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli powder, garam masala, lemon juice, and oil. The Achari variant incorporates a pickling spice blend (achari masala) featuring mustard seeds, fennel seeds, nigella seeds, fenugreek, and dried mango powder (amchur), giving it a tangy, piquant flavour. The Hariyali variant uses a vibrant green herb paste of fresh mint, coriander leaves, and green chillies, producing a herbaceous, mildly spicy profile. All three variants are typically skewered with bell peppers, onion, and tomato before grilling, which adds sweetness and char. The finished dish has a firm, slightly smoky exterior with a soft, creamy interior. Nutritionally, paneer tikka is high in protein (approximately 14 g per 100 g) and calcium (approximately 390 mg per 100 g), making it a valuable protein source for vegetarians. Its fat content is moderate to high due to full-fat paneer, with saturated fat being the primary concern for cardiovascular health. The glycemic index is low (approximately 27), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is elevated due to the marinade and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, paneer tikka is served at Indian restaurants and is popular as a starter or as part of a mixed grill platter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in paneer tikka?
Is paneer tikka good for diabetics?
What is the difference between achari and hariyali paneer tikka?
How much protein in paneer tikka?
What spices are used in paneer tikka marinade?
Is paneer tikka high in sodium?
Can I eat paneer tikka if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN grilled paneer data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central; no single approved source provides a direct paneer tikka composite entry. GI is an analogous estimate from published fresh paneer GI data. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: This record covers three preparation variants of paneer tikka served as a combined menu item: standard tikka, achari (pickle-spiced), and hariyali (green herb). Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for the marinated-and-grilled product per 100 g edible portion, adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) grilled paneer values and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 2346428). Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central paneer data. GI of 27 is an analogous estimate based on published GI for fresh paneer/Indian cottage cheese (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care; University of Sydney GI Database). GL per serving = (27 × 14.5) / 100 = 3.9, rounded to 4.0. The food is classified as Indian cuisine; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. The restaurant source (Dugout - Circular Road, field survey data) is not used as a nutritional evidence source per Project Veritas source policy.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi as required for Indian cuisine in Singapore context.
Adapted nutrient values from ICMR-NIN grilled paneer data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-gap justification in notes.
Used analogous GI estimate (GI 27) from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 for fresh paneer.
Verified aliases_by_language entries are all present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to allowed values: exact, closest_parent, or none.
Provided specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including quantified nutrient thresholds.
